text.title: Python Web Framework Decision Matrix
text.summary: When to choose Django, FastAPI, or Flask, with concrete decision rules mapped to project requirements.

rule.statement: Choose Django for full-stack apps with admin panels, ORM, and batteries-included security. Lock the choice in constitution.md before writing code.
rule.rationale: Django's built-in admin saves weeks of development for content management. Switching frameworks after models and migrations exist is extremely costly.

rule.statement: Choose FastAPI for high-concurrency async APIs, ML model serving, and microservices where native async and auto-generated OpenAPI are priorities.
rule.rationale: FastAPI's async-native design and Pydantic validation pipeline yield faster throughput and automatic schema documentation that stays in sync with the code.

rule.statement: Choose Flask for small internal tools, rapid prototypes, and cases where maximum flexibility and minimal overhead are priorities.
rule.rationale: Flask's minimal core means less startup cost, but security, validation, and structure must be added manually — avoid it for production APIs that will grow.

list.item: Full-stack web app with HTML templates → Django
Admin panel or CMS needed → Django
ML model serving / OpenAPI auto-docs → FastAPI
Quick prototype or internal tool → Flask

section.title: Framework Core Characteristics
section.p: Django: MTV architecture, built-in ORM, admin, migrations, auth, CSRF/XSS protection, partial ASGI. Real-world: Instagram, Spotify, Dropbox.
FastAPI: dependency injection, native async, Pydantic v2 validation, auto OpenAPI, SQLAlchemy 2 async. Real-world: Netflix, Uber, Microsoft.
Flask: WSGI micro-framework, Jinja2, extension ecosystem. Real-world: Airbnb, Reddit (legacy).
